skill.name: crafting-effective-readmes
skill.description: Helps write, review, and update README files with templates matched to project type and audience.
skill.summary: Create clearer README files for open source, personal, internal, and configuration projects.
skill.author: softaworks

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, URL, secret, weak-crypto, and heuristic findings, but the reviewed files are Markdown guidance and templates. The command and network matches are documentation examples or links, and no malicious intent, active execution, credential exfiltration, or prompt injection was found.

low_findings.title: Repository File Review Is Expected
low_findings.description: The skill asks the assistant to read the current README and compare it with project files such as package metadata and main files. This is normal for README review, but users should expect repository content to be inspected during documentation updates.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file-reading behavior is explicitly documented and directly tied to the README review workflow. I found no evidence of hidden collection, persistence, or transmission of file contents.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Variable Names Are Template Placeholders
low_findings.description: The internal README template includes placeholder rows for DATABASE_URL and API_KEY and references a secrets manager. These are not real credentials, but users should document names and storage locations only, not secret values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious strings appear inside a Markdown table for environment variable documentation. They are placeholders, not code that reads environment variables or exfiltrates secrets.

low_findings.title: Static Command Matches Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The external command findings are backticked filenames, code fence labels, package-manager examples, or template placeholders. They do not execute commands and do not create a command-injection path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple flagged lines are plain Markdown literals or fenced examples. I found no executable scripts, shell wrappers, dynamic command construction, or user-input execution.

low_findings.title: Static Network Matches Are Reference Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings are citations, badge examples, and documentation links. They are not network requests made by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are embedded in Markdown text and badge syntax. I found no fetch, curl, webhook, telemetry, or code path that sends data to those URLs.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Match Is a Documentation Link
low_findings.description: The path traversal finding is a relative Markdown link to a license file in a sample README. It is not used for file access, writes, or path construction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The ../LICENSE sequence appears only as link text in an example license section. There is no runtime filesystem operation using this path.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Matches Are README Terminology
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography findings are false positives caused by README-related terms, Markdown extensions, licenses, and prose. No cryptographic implementation or algorithm selection is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged contexts are Markdown metadata, README naming guidance, and placeholder tables. I found no hashing, encryption, signing, or security-sensitive algorithm use.

content.value_statement: README files often miss the needs of their real audience. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through project-specific templates and review steps.
actual_capabilities: Selects README guidance for open source, personal, internal, and configuration projects.
Provides templates for common README structures and section ordering.
Guides creation, updates, additions, and review workflows.
Checks whether existing README content matches current project files.
Uses reference material for README style, section choices, and standard-readme compliance.
limitations: Does not verify external links unless the assistant is allowed to browse or test them.
Does not replace project owner judgment about audience, roadmap, or support policies.
May need access to repository files to review README accuracy.
Templates contain placeholders that must be replaced with project-specific details.

use_cases.title: Launch an open source library
use_cases.description: Create a README with installation, usage, contribution, and license sections for public users and contributors.

use_cases.title: Onboard teammates to an internal service
use_cases.description: Draft internal documentation with setup steps, architecture notes, runbooks, and operational contacts.

use_cases.title: Document a personal configuration directory
use_cases.description: Explain what files do, why the setup exists, how to extend it, and what future maintenance risks exist.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic README
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for a new project
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a README for this project. Ask me what type of project it is, who will read it, and the quickest path to make it work.

prompt_templates.title: Improve an existing README
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the current README and suggest focused improvements. Check whether setup, usage, and examples still match the project.

prompt_templates.title: Add a targeted section
prompt_templates.prompt: Add a section about the new feature I describe. Place it where readers would expect it and match the existing README style.

prompt_templates.title: Produce an audience-specific README plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this repository and propose a README structure for primary users, contributors, and maintainers. Explain which sections to include or omit.

output_examples.input: A maintainer asks for a README for a new public package.
output: Recommended sections for a public package.
A concise draft with install, usage, contribution, and license sections.
Follow-up questions for missing package name, command examples, and support policy.

output_examples.input: A team asks whether an internal service README is current.
output: A list of stale or missing sections.
Suggested edits for setup, environment variables, runbooks, and related docs.
A note about project files that should be checked before publishing the update.

output_examples.input: A developer wants documentation for a dotfiles folder.
output: A README outline focused on future maintenance.
Tables for important paths and dependencies.
Plain notes for gotchas, sync behavior, and last reviewed date.

best_practices: Start by identifying the reader and the task before choosing a template.
Include the fastest working path, then add deeper detail only where useful.
Review README content against real project files before calling it current.
anti_patterns: Using the open source template for every project without checking the audience.
Documenting secret values instead of variable names and storage locations.
Writing broad claims without install steps, examples, or maintenance notes.

faq.question: What types of README files does this skill support?
faq.answer: It supports open source projects, personal projects, internal tools, and configuration directories.

faq.question: Can it update an existing README?
faq.answer: Yes. It can review current content, identify stale sections, and propose focused edits.

faq.question: Does it inspect repository files?
faq.answer: It may inspect project files when reviewing accuracy, such as package metadata, main files, and existing README content.

faq.question: Does it include ready-made templates?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes templates for open source, personal, internal, and configuration documentation.

faq.question: Is it only for Claude Code?
faq.answer: No. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Should secret values be included in generated READMEs?
faq.answer: No. Document variable names and where to get access, but never include actual credentials.
